summary: Design for a read-only `dscli status` subcommand that lists all datasystem worker processes running on the local host. Discovery reuses the proven `pgrep -fl -- '-worker_address='` mechanism from `stop.py`; per-PID worker addresses are extracted from `/proc/<pid>/cmdline`. A new `cli/status.py` module follows the `BaseCommand` pattern, and `cli/command.py` is extended with a one-line registration. Exit code 0 in all success cases (including zero workers); exit code 1 only on tool errors.

ledger.what: List all workers on the host, not a single worker by address
ledger.why: Issue says 'list running datasystem workers'; no address filter was requested
ledger.what_i_did: Designed `status` to take no arguments and enumerate all matching PIDs

reversibility.why: An optional -w/--worker_address flag can be layered on later without breaking the no-arg behavior

ledger.revisit_if: A specific-worker check is needed (e.g., readiness probe for a single address)

ledger.why: `stop.py` already uses `pgrep` for process detection; the `-worker_address=` flag is the canonical worker identifier; avoids pulling in a new `psutil` dependency
ledger.what_i_did: Mirrored the pgrep-based approach from stop.py:263

reversibility.why: Switch to psutil if needed; the change is isolated to _list_workers()

ledger.revisit_if: `pgrep` is removed from the supported environment or the team decides to standardise on `psutil`

ledger.what: Extract worker address from `/proc/<pid>/cmdline` per discovered PID
ledger.why: `pgrep -fl` outputs only the binary name, not full args; `pgrep -fa` (full command line) is a Linux-only extension not available on all distributions; reading `/proc/<pid>/cmdline` is the most portable Linux-native approach
ledger.what_i_did: status.py reads /proc/<pid>/cmdline, splits on null bytes, and extracts the -worker_address= token

reversibility.why: Can swap to `ps -p <pid> -o args=` or psutil.Process(pid).cmdline() without changing the public interface

ledger.revisit_if: The system is ported to macOS or another OS where /proc is absent; at that point switch to `ps -p <pid> -o args=` or `psutil.Process(pid).cmdline()`

ledger.what: `pgrep` is present on all supported deployment hosts
ledger.why: stop.py:263 invokes pgrep unconditionally; the project already depends on it
ledger.what_i_did: Accepted pgrep as an existing dependency; no new runtime requirement introduced

blast_radius.why: Discovery falls back gracefully to empty list if pgrep errors

ledger.revisit_if: The deployment target changes to a minimal container image that does not include procps-ng

ledger.what: Plain-text table output to stdout via logger.info
ledger.why: Matches the [INFO] logging pattern used by all other commands; no JSON or structured output was requested
ledger.what_i_did: Designed output as a two-column table (PID, ADDRESS) printed line-by-line via the existing BaseCommand logger

blast_radius.why: Output format has no downstream side effects

reversibility.why: A --json flag can be added later without breaking the default plain-text output

ledger.revisit_if: Operators need machine-parseable output for integration with monitoring pipelines

ledger.what: Exit with code 0 when no workers are found
ledger.why: status is an informational command; a non-zero exit for 'no workers' would break shell scripts that use it for inventory and expect a clean exit when the system is idle
ledger.what_i_did: Designed run() to print a 'no workers' message and return SUCCESS (0) in the zero-count case

blast_radius.why: Exit code semantics only affect calling scripts; no system state is mutated

reversibility.why: Exit code semantics are easy to change; no external contract is published yet

ledger.revisit_if: Operators or CI scripts require a non-zero exit code to distinguish 'zero workers running' from 'command succeeded with workers present' (e.g., for health-check probes that must fail when the system is idle); at that point add a --require-running flag

ledger.what: The pattern `-worker_address=` uniquely identifies datasystem worker processes on the host
ledger.why: stop.py:261 constructs `target_arg = f'-worker_address={address}'` and uses it as the sole process selector; if it were not unique the existing stop command would already be broken
ledger.what_i_did: Reused the same pattern for status; verified against source: target/cli/stop.py:261 — `target_arg = f'-worker_address={address}'`

blast_radius.why: A false match would only cause an extra row in status output, not a destructive action

reversibility.why: The pattern string is in one constant in _list_workers(); easy to change

ledger.revisit_if: A future worker binary renames the flag (e.g., to --listen_addr or --address), or a third-party process begins using `-worker_address=` in its arguments causing false matches or misses
